sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Coronal graphic illustrates swirling acute hemorrhage from a laceration of the middle meningeal artery (MMA) by an overlying skull fracture. The swirl sign suggests active, rapid bleeding and is CT hypodense. The epidural hematoma (EDH) displaces the dura inward as it expands between the dura and the skull.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-01

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT, bone window shows a comminuted, depressed fracture of the left squamous temporal bone. There is marked swelling of the overlying scalp.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-02

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in the same patient shows the comminuted, depressed skull fracture is associated with an acute EDH (aEDH) . This classic EDH is hyperdense and has a typical biconvex, lens-shaped configuration. Note the focal subgaleal hematoma , indicating the impact site.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-03

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Coronal NECT in the same patient shows the aEDH as a biconvex collection causing local mass effect on the adjacent brain with adjacent sulcal effacement . aEDH was evacuated at surgery for the depressed skull fracture.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-04

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT shows an aEDH ; note focus of calcium displaced to the inner margin. Minimal mass effect was associated with the aEDH, so the patient was placed in the ER observation unit.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-05

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Patient deteriorated 3 hours later. Repeat NECT shows growth of the EDH with a swirl sign . Surgery disclosed an actively bleeding hematoma between the periosteal and meningeal layers of the dura. Traumatic interdural hematomas are a rare variant, often associated with displaced dural calcifications.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-06

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Coronal CTA head shows a venous EDH from a lacerated superior sagittal sinus. The dura and superior sagittal sinus are displaced away from the calvarium. The EDH crosses the suture line into the contralateral side . CTA nicely illustrates the displacement of the dura and the major dural venous sinuses away from the skull.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-07

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial bone CT in a young adult trauma patient demonstrates a subtle nondisplaced fracture of the right frontal bone and diastasis of the sagittal suture .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-08

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in a trauma patient shows an aEDH ; note the swirl sign and a focus of air within the hematoma, indicating a fractured paranasal sinus. Marked mass effect was associated with the hematoma, so the patient underwent surgery, which showed copious bleeding from a branch of the ethmoidal artery.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-09

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial bone CT of the same patient demonstrates a nondisplaced fracture of the right frontal sinus inner wall , which explains the focus of air within the aEDH.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-10

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in a 47-year-old man with head trauma shows a classic biconvex (lentiform) uniformly hyperdense EDH in the right middle fossa. Bone CT (not shown) disclosed a nondisplaced skull fracture underlying the hematoma.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-11

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Coronal NECT reformatted from the axial date nicely demonstrates the classic middle cranial fossa aEDH .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-12

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Sagittal NECT reformatted from the axial source date in the same patient shows the classic biconvex configuration of the middle fossa aEDH is present in all 3 major imaging planes.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-13

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: NECT 5 weeks later shows the EDH is decreasing in size and density.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-14

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT shows a left temporal convexity EDH . Note the medial displacement of the uncus , indicative of early transtentorial herniation.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-15

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT demonstrates an EDH crossing the falx attachment site , distinguishing it from a subdural hematoma. A hemorrhagic cerebral contusion is also present.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-16

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Large mixed-density aEDH indicates rapid active bleeding. Note transtentorial herniation of left temporal uncus and hippocampus .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-17

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial T2 MR demonstrates linear hypointense dura interposed between an EDH and the underlying brain. This distinguishes EDH from subdural hematoma.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-18

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT shows a patient with posterior fossa aEDH ; note the swirl sign within the hematoma and a focus of air within the right sigmoid sinus, indicating fractured mastoid air cells. Marked mass effect was associated with the hematoma, so the patient underwent surgery, which showed continuous oozing from the right sigmoid sinus.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-19

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial bone CT of the same patient demonstrates a nondisplaced fracture of the right mastoid air cells , which explains the adjacent focus of air and the injury to the right sigmoid sinus.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-20

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in the same case reveals a small biconvex EDH underlying the skull fracture. The aEDH is mixed density with a less-dense center (swirl sign), possibly indicating active hemorrhage. There is also a posterior falcine subdural hematoma tracking along the tentorium and superior sagittal sinus .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-21

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial bone CT demonstrates a portion of a comminuted, slightly depressed fracture of the right squamous temporal bone adjacent to the pterion .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-22

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Coronal T2 MR shows a venous EDH from a lacerated superior sagittal sinus. The dura and superior sagittal sinus are displaced. The skull fracture is barely visible.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-23

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Sagittal MR venography demonstrates mass effect from a vertex venous EDH that displaces the superior sagittal sinus.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-24

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: NECT following acute head trauma shows a small right parietal aEDH . Only mild mass effect on the underlying brain and lateral ventricle is present. The patient's Glasgow Coma Scale was 13, so no surgery was performed.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-25

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Repeat scan 10 days later shows the hematoma is unchanged in size, but its density has decreased significantly. Small bifrontal subdural hygromas are present. Repeat study 6 weeks after trauma (not shown) disclosed complete resolution of the EDH and subdural hygromas.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-26

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT shows a hyperdense, biconvex EDH with compression of the brain. Note the internal hypodense swirl sign , which implies active bleeding with an unretracted, semiliquid clot.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-27

downloadSentinel:
Core Validation Gate satisfied using the uploaded Core Radiology PDF, Neuroimaging: Brain/Cerebral Trauma, pages labeled Neuro Brain 701–702. Core facts used included arterial EDH mechanism, lentiform/suture-limited shape, swirl sign, surgical-vs-conservative pivot, and venous EDH exceptions.

Whole-source coverage audit completed from full Core pages plus the fused RadPrimer/STATdx source package.
Total cards to export: 40
Expected TSV lines: 40
Validation passed: 40 physical TSV lines, 22 columns per line.

anki.deckName: Corebook::Neuro::Brain::Primary Effects of CNS Trauma::Epidural Hematoma, Classic

imageRegistry.caption: Coronal graphic illustrates swirling acute hemorrhage from a laceration of the middle meningeal artery by an overlying skull fracture. The epidural hematoma (EDH) displaces the dura inward as it expands.
